speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Edward Groseclose and Dixie Groseclose took place at their home in Juliaetta on March 9, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: A little town, same neighborhood, but he was nine when he came and I was seven when I come. I'm only 76, he's seven years older than I. But his folks came west before mine did and we got to learn to know each other, went to school together, ended up getting married, been married 59 years now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did your folks come right to Arrow Junction too?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they know each other from back there pretty well? What were they figuring on when they come out, that they were going to farm or what?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, I don't know your folks.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: This came west because they were looking for more land, wasn't it Ed or what?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, that's what we, there's homestead land in this country here then. Most all of them homesteaded.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I wonder how they happened to pick Arrow Junction. Well, now it wasn't Arrow Junction then, it was Potlatch. Potlatch Junction. Potlatch Junction.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And, well, to tell what caused them to be that way, my oldest brothers come to this country looking for good cattle country.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And they landed up here at Oakesdale and they went to search in the country from there down and they come in, come down in here in about 1900, the older brothers. And they went down the top of that hill there in January and there was two feet of snow up there. And they dropped down in the canyon and there wasn't no snow in there at all. And it was one of these years that seemed to be that we had good early rain in the fall and the grass was great big clear, they topped their shoes down in there and wild horses, fat and nice. Homestead land, they figured that was the place they were looking for. Well, you know, there's been about ten years since that that there's been good grass here in the fall.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: This time it's been pretty dry. Lots of times it didn't amount to any grass at all. Well, it's free up, you know, before anything grows. But this right here now is one spring that's awful bad holding stuff back. I believe it's one of the worst we've had for being this far in March. You know, nothing has started to grow. Winter's been nice, but then...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were your brothers out here then before your father?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, they were here first. Yeah, I had three brothers here that had homesteads when we come out here. They told them about the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did they get their homesteads? Where did they take up their homesteads around here?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, my brother Will took up a homestead in Catholic Gulch, where Margaret Smith lives. That's where that was his homestead there. Smith bought more land than what he had around there, but then he had 160 that where Margaret lives, that's where her house is, that's where his was. Then my oldest brother, his homestead was where that pile of junk is, over there by the slicker pools. About 4 or 5 miles below town. It's about 3 1 3 miles below town here. Gene Groseclose has got, he's so young, his son has got part of the place there now. He's got a house partly built over there on it. Gene's got 40 acres out of the 160.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So there was still homestead to be taken around here when they came out?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh yes, at that time. Still wild horses running on the range when they came here. Well, the Groseclose is kind of the sport that. The horses run on the top of that potlatch ridge up there.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: My brother Albert, his homestead is down there where that great big walnut tree is above the road, about 6 miles below here. That's great big walnut, right above the road. He planted that there in 1903. That's when that tree was planted there.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: My brother Marion, he come west with us, and my sister, they was old enough to take claims. My brother went and looked at a place up on top of the potlatch ridge. Before he got around to homesteaded, old Tommy Summers took it. So then he took a place a mile long down there and he got that nice bench that's over across the potlatch. That was where he got that.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: You mean at Albright?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, Albright said that's what wolves got over across. And then he had about 60 acres on this side of the river. Down there where they've got them trailer houses now set up above the road there. That was where his homestead house was, right there. So he came out with you and your parents. Yes, he come.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So this would have been what, 1901?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: 1902. 1902. Boy, I'm surprised there was still homesteading to be had right around here. I'm surprised. Then my sister, she got the place where Raleigh Albright's got down there. There was a little piece of ground showed up there. Well, the road at that time went right below that old house that's down there. Went right below that and over and around that hill. Went around that bluff all the way around there.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: You know where the Albright place is?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, I do. Well, where the Albright's house is down there, that was on the place that Floyd Leach got. He took out all that bluff up there. But that was where her house is, it was on his. But it's just a few rods below where my sister's place went to there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what about you? You were still a kid and living with your folks?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I was just a kid. I was too young for that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did your folks move to? Where did they take? Did they take a homestead?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, my dad, he thought he would get a homestead down there where that bluff is across the river. All that long bluff and the little potlatch up there. Well, the Indians turned in and put their fences over this way. So it made it look like that there was a lot of land over there next to the potlatch. So he took the claim in there. But when they surveyed it out, they were scared of anything but the river and that bluff. So he turned it loose again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was all land? Was this all land that got opened up after they opened up the reservation?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That was the reservation. Then the homestead again, up in the hills, back up in the timber country, they got a wrong location for the corner. They thought they found a good piece of ground and he homesteaded. And when they surveyed it out, it was another bluff as bad as that there, so he turned it loose again. He never kept it. He bought a place up on the fixed ridge here, 120 acres, and kept that for quite a number of years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These Indians had turned it so that it didn't look like it was fixed there?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, they'd just run their fences on further up the hill than what they're supposed to. And from where their fences were up against the hill, the length of their place left a lot of land over there next to the potlatch. But when they surveyed it out, their places went closer to the river, so there wasn't nothing in there worth anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where he bought on fixed ridge, was that mostly cleared?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That was farmland.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had that land on fixed ridge, had that all been bunch grass to start with?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh yes, long years ago that was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So did he have much of that he was growing crop on?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That was all broke out before he got to pass. The thing that really interested the brothers when they come, they could have had land on top of the hill, farmland more. No, they couldn't have had a bought it right on top. So it was a good winter and there was apples still hanging on the trees that weren't frozen, and green grass tall and everything, so they thought they'd come to the Garden of Eden so they'd all settle down this canyon. Well now then, they're having a lot of argument now that makes me awful mad about protecting these wild animals. Maybe he doesn't want that, he wants something else. Well, you want to talk about the early days, which I want to talk about most. That'll lead back to the early days. When old man Fix, he was the first man that come on the fixed ridge up here. The prairie chickens, oh there was about a thousand up there. And he went out there and made him a box trap with a trip board, and he put a sheaf of grain up there. And the birds would come out to get that on the end of that board, you know, and they'd drop them in the box. They come a little slow and Sam Tabor, stepson of his, he was only about 14 at that time, he went out to see about the trap one morning, come a running back, he said, run out here quick, Paul, the birds are flying away with that trap. He just took the full, you know, and they're just about to tear it off. So that's the way the thing was when the white man come here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to all them prairie chickens?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, now that's it. The white men killed out an awful lot of them and then, well there was a peculiar bird. When you scared them up, they'd just fly over the next hill in light, just out of sight. Well, men could follow a bunch of them all day if you wanted to. They wouldn't fly over half a mile, you know, just from the top of one row, or just out of sight on the top of the other one, you'd follow them. Well, there wasn't so very many up there, oh, quite a lot too on the ridge when we went up there in 1904, but then the squirrels got so bad that the farmers put out poison, and that cleaned the prairie chickens up entirely. Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the poison not so much the hunting that did them in, huh?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, there was an awful lot of them gone in before they finished them up by poison.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why has somebody said something like that to me?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I'm interested that you say that too. Yeah. Well, the first man, one of the oldest men in the country, located over there on the Genesee, was the old man Gibb.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You see these trails around these hills here on the rocks?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: We call them Cayuse trails when they come here, but Gibb called them deer trails. Yeah, he called them deer trails. People who only come here said that there was no buzzards in this country. Gibb was the first man here he said there was, but the white man, the first ones that come here in order to get rid of the coyotes, they poisoned dead horses, and that's what the buzzards were eating. Well, they killed all the buzzards, but the coyotes, they got pretty wise. They finally got wise until they didn't. Well, they wouldn't eat a poisoned horse. They learned better than that. And the magpie eats another thing. That's awful hard to poison. There were lots of them here, and we learned how to poison them. When you butcher, when you poison magpies, when you butcher gets a blood, a considerable bucket of blood, let it clot, and then put stricken in it and hang it up on a post in the fence. And them birds, them magpies will rush to that, but don't let them stay there. Don't let them stay there five minutes. Get down there and chase them away. And they'll keep coming back. If you let one get sick there, he'll get sick, wank, wank, wank, wank, wank, wank, wank, wank, wank, wank, wank, wank, wank, wank, wank. You'll never get another magpie. They understand what they're saying. And you'll never get another magpie if one ever gets sick where your poison is. But if you keep them scared away, well, you can just keep taking them. They drink it right out of the bucket. Yeah, they just, that clotted blood, you know, they just eat that. Oh, yeah, you could, but they was here, oh, they was here by the thousands when we come here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they do much damage?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, did they. They would steal the eggs out from under a setting hen. They'd steal the little chickens. And, well, they was just a terrible bird. And in order to get rid of them, why, they went, ever spraying out, and burnt all the nests you could find all over the country. Just about the time, just before they hatched, they'd burn them all up. And shoot anything you've got close enough. But they're wise about that gun business. They'll give you plenty of room when they know, when they know you've got a gun out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did all this stuff cut down the size of the population very much? Did they cut down the numbers? The magpies?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, yes, there's very few in the country now. Compared to what there was back then. Oh, well, I've seen them light on a big thornbrush. It'd be enough on it they'd make it bend. There'd be a thousand magpies on a thornbrush. Now then, I don't suppose you could find 20 in a bunch anywhere in the country. They ain't scared of any.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you came into this canyon, was the kind of trees, and the way it was grown on the land was pretty much the way it is now, was it very different?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Quite a lot the same as it is now. There wasn't much timber, very little. A little more along the potlatch. That potlatch has whipped across there. It used to run right over by the railroad. And the railroad come in there, and you see that made it stay on the other side of the railroad. Sometimes in a big flood, it would come clear over and spread all over that flat down through there. But when the railroad made it stay over there, that started to cut. So instead of being over next to the railroad, now it's over against the other hill. It just worked that whole flat down through there over. All right, down here below town where Sherman's Barn is, there was a good nice pear orchard on the other side of the river there. When we come here, quite a bunch of trees in there in that flat. The potlatch just kept moving over and took them all out, went clear over against the hill, made them move the houses over there over further. They finally built a house way up on the hill, and then they sold it out. There's nobody over there at all anymore. Well, there was quite a lot of orchards here. There were a lot of orchards here right then, it turns out. Yes, there was. There was no orchards. More later years, still. Down here where Bill Carton lives, that flat in there at his place was a big apple orchard, large trees then. And over across, that was all the same place. That was prunes and pears over there. But it's all been pulled out, pulled the orchard out down there, apple orchard, and they put it in cherries. And they growed up and they pulled them out and he's gardening again now since I come here.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, it's got to be compared to up on them ridges and over towards Troy. It must have been just great for growing fruit back then here. Yeah, well, every farm on the hill used to have an orchard. This Hutcheson, Jim Hutcheson's place up here, he had a 40 -acre prune orchard up there. Forty -eight years. Well, it wasn't quite all prunes. He had apples and cherries. There's some other stuff in there, but he had a 40 -acre orchard there. He had a prune dryer. Then there was another 40 -acre orchard over here on the Potlatch Ridge. I forget who had it, but it's all pulled out now. There's nothing there. But the seasons have changed enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How have they changed?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, they seem to be colder or something. Back then, you could plant garden in February down in the canyon here. We could have garden, like lettuce and onions and stuff like that, big enough to eat before 25 miles back at Teakin. We lived there for a while. We could mayo garden stuff and gather it. On the morning, they'd put it in the mail, they'd get it the next day. We could send stuff up to them because it would be so much earlier. But now it's getting so that they can have it just about as quick as we can. It just seemed like the seasons are... Well, there was more timber up there then, and that held the snow longer than it was now. They've got that cleared up in there. Snow goes out lots quicker now than it did then. But it's not often that you can plant anything down here in February anymore. But there are lots of times way back then you planted your early stuff even in February.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How often would you get snow here in the winter in those early days?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, now that's... Let's see, the first year, 1904. My brother Will went east and got married at four and five. My brother Grover stayed down there, his place, and went to school spalling. And they snowed, played snowball two mornings at recess all winter. That was all the snow they had. There's one winter they had this deep snow here that a fellow here in Juliaetta claimed they had five feet of snow. Five feet of snow fall. He got it off a barrel, you know, and he'd clean it off every morning and then measure it every morning and then clean it off. Well, five feet of snow fall is not five feet of packed snow by a lot. It'll take anyway ten feet of snow to make five foot of packed snow. And that's what they used to have up there in the hills, that snow packed clear over top of the fence post so you could get out there and drive a team over top of a fence.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: We never had the snow down here like, you know, up on the hill. No, they never had much. But the snow that winter, I was feeding some horses and cattle and goats. And it was about 30 inches underground. And it was soft. I'd ride down there. You couldn't trust the snow to get right out and ride on it. Your horse liable to break through. It was too soft. You had to take it easy.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Then the railroad here, well, it was, we say the railroad, the wagon road crossed the railroad eight times between here and Spalding. But it was really the other way. The road was there first.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Down here where Bill Carton lives, it crossed twice there and went over next to the river, across the track next to the river and then come back down further down there. And then down there where that old schoolhouse is, it crossed up above that and then come out, well, it come out first down there about where Johnson's Barn is down there. But the river washed it out and then they changed it back up. There was a little rock cliff down there. Oh, it wasn't 50 feet through, but it run out there 50 or 75 feet. Just that little narrow ridge of rock. Well, that's why the railroad is over where it is because that little piece of rock was there and in order to not blast that out, they missed. They just passed it up. Well, then the road got cut out so much, so far back, till they finally got in and the highway outfit cut that cliff off and put the road up the other side of the railroad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did your folks, did your father get involved there at all at Arrow Junction at the depot or anything? Or was that just you that did that?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I understand you became the depot. No, he wasn't. I worked on the railroad there for 20 years. He was just.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you start working on the railroad? About when?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: About 38. Oh, he was 20 years on there. Well, you were pretty, you had already grown quite a bit, grown by then. Oh, yes. I was old enough that it was kind of a joke the way I got on there. They didn't want a man 45 years old. They wanted him to be laid off or get rid of him some way that he didn't make rights, but that was right along about wartime. And there was a fella. Well, no, the first thing, they made a relay and they wanted a man there for about 10 days. Then when this man was done, why, there was a man got sick and I stayed on and worked till he come back. And then there was a, another one went on vacation and I worked his time while he was gone. Then I thought I was done. I was off for about 10 days and one of the other men fell over on the job. And he died before they got him to the hospital. So I went back and worked till they laid off, cut the crew in the fall. And you're supposed to have 163 days to make rights. And I had 153 when I got laid off. And they were a carload of lumber, I think it was, jumped the tracks up there below Peck and run about three quarters of a mile down on the ties and just cut the road all to pieces. So they got all the men that they could to go and straighten that track up. And when they got that, I got on that, and when they got that done I had 173 days. So that means you were, you were... I had rights when... He just barely made it though by his work. When they increased the force, I had to be called in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He had to make it before a certain time?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: You had to make that in a year. I had to work 163 days a year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then you had to make it before you was 45 or in the year you were 45?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, you had to make it. Well, anytime you made that when they worked you on the railroad, anytime you made that, why, that was, you had your rights. But they didn't want a man that old. They'd, for some reason or other, they'd have him laid off or something so he wouldn't get on. But that, I just got through that. No, I worked with a young fellow after that there. He was a good young man. And they took him on during tie season. That's when they put on a lot of men, you know, when they put in ties. And he worked for five years during tie season and then was laid off before he made rides.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Then when the war came on, wages went to raisins for every place else. But the railroad wouldn't raise wages. So the good young men that they had, they went for something else. They just jumped out of that and went for somewhere else. The railroad company finally said, no, they wouldn't give them any more per hour per day, but they'd work ten hours and give them eleven hours pay. Well, you know, that hurt me. I know that the men on there would have done more work in eight hours than they did in the ten if they had just get rid of their wages. But that's something they didn't do that. Then you had to go on and... When he retired, he had worked by people quitting and changing around and dying and everything. He worked up from way down to the bottom until he was first man. I was first man at that time. Well, there was twelve laborers older than me on the section when I quit. The three of them was young men and the rest was old. That was on the whole division. On the whole railroad division. There was only twelve laborers older that had more rights than me. When it started out, I suppose they must have been fifty or sixty or something. Oh, there was a whole bunch of them. Any time that you had more rights, half a day more rights than another fellow, anywhere on that line when you got laid off, cut your force at your section, anywhere on that division you could bump and take the job away from any man that had a half a day's less time in than you did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that happen much?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, yes, it happened quite a lot. He even bumped out a time or two when he got bumped to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a union at the time?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, oh yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the union pretty good?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, it was good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a union in 38 or did that come in later?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, it was here at 38. I was always a union man.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He was here when, I mean, we were living in the section house, well the depot, and they had the bad train wrecks, and Eddie Finn, and engineer. Oh yeah, I was in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened there?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: The logging train was going up. Up the river. Up the river. And another train come up and falling around. There was kind of a double curve there. And this other train was coming up there and this logger was stopped. And this train, when it come around there, was too close to stop. And it hit that logger. Cut Eddie Finn. Hit the caboose, where you go. Hit the caboose. The fellow across the river said that caboose just exploded. Went all to pieces. Well, it mashed about six cars up too. Just crushed them right up together. And jumped two diesel motors were stowed off the tracks. And then it caught a fire. They had three pumps in the river before they got the fire out. Burnt Finn and a feehen awful bad there. His body was cutting too. And then there was two others killed and another one maxed up pretty bad there in that wreck. And that was just blow where we were living. I wasn't home, but he was the first one to the wreck. I was sitting in the house. I heard the train sounded like it was just a pulling out. And somebody come up and asked me if that was a depot. And I told them it was. And said they had a wreck. Well, I said I better get down there then. See what about it. I was the only second railroad man that went along there, you know. So I went down and looked where the outfit was over. I worked for better than 24 hours straight on that job there without any sleep.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you know, could you tell pretty quick, was everybody on the train hurt or killed?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, it was just the ones, well the engineer outfit. And the conductor on the logging train. And he was killed and the engineer and the fireman I guess on the other one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to try to get any of the men out of the train?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, I didn't have the ones that was able to get out got out. They didn't have to pick anybody up till.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what caused that anyway?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, it was just that other train coming too fast around them turns and got so close that it couldn't stop. To get out into that. But Finn had told the fellas there was some cars that set out. Some cars set out. Some cars set out on the lower end of that siding down there. That end of that siding. And Finn had told his men two days before that, don't put any more cars, any cars down there because that's a trap. You get them cars down there and the train stopped behind them and another train can't see it and they'll hit it. He told them that but somebody else come and set a bunch of cars down there. And he was the man that got caught, one of the men that got caught in the trap. He had warned his men not to put cars down there anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You having the cars down there you couldn't see the train behind it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, you couldn't see the train behind it. You come around that turn. The section foreman laughed about, of course they all were, there's lots of men that worked straight through like he did. But he worked until they got the engine set up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he didn't say anything, asked the boss can I go home?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: The boss laughed about it. He said, entered down his shovel and says I'm going home. He said he took off of the track, wasn't very far home but 24 hours or so was all he. You know, the rest of them were just tired I guess too but he was about the first one that just throwed down and says I'm going.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And they fixed supper for the, I was gone at the time but our daughters heard about the wreck and they went home. One of them went up in the store and they fixed sandwiches and they had coffee and had lunch for the men coming in all night long. You know, they kept food there but they were close. I'm wondering about when that wreck did take place, about which year that would have been.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, what year was that?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That's when our memory, I've got the papers on it but I don't know exactly where they're at.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It doesn't matter but would it have been after the Second World War?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, Johnny was big enough to drive the pickup to all of our houses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes, sure, why the Second World War?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I told John it would have been about 13, 14. He's 39. I look in that one book and see. Oh, that's okay. It looks like maybe 2025. He was out on the track tonight but the engine went through the bridge down here too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Engine went through the bridge?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, yes, that was another time. Potlatch got on a rampage and tore the railroad here and all the pieces down through there. There was a fellow Skidmore, he was a fireman.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They had every man that they could get. The track was washed out and they were putting in a dirt field. The river started raising again, cutting that field until they got every man that they could find. They had them down there filling sacks, sandbagging all that field. This night train come down and this little creek there above era took a little raise. The bridge had been washed out around there and I got a job with a crew down there and helped fill that in there with just loose dirt. That bridge there, where that bridge is, down there at Wings, just above era there. That little creek raised again and cut this dirt out from under there. So there wasn't nothing under there, only just the dirt in the track. The engineer couldn't see that and they drove right out on that. The engine tipped over into the potlatch and drowned at this Skidmore. I don't know for sure whether they ever did find his body or not. If they did, it was several years after that. But he passed by the crew up there and they stopped sandbagging right at the time they came along. The river started dropping but they were still holding the crew there. He came by and he was laughing and talking with the men there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, hello boys, how do you like it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: This is railroading. Then he just went down there and went in the river. I wasn't there before it got dark. I had seen a new tie laying off over across there. So as the train came down, I used the lights for the train to go get that tie to put in the fire that we had there to stand by. I was off over there when the train went through. I brought the tie back in the dark but I went over to it in the light of the train.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You saw the train go through?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: The train went through where we was at. That was down here at Allbrash. That's where we were working at that night. But it was strange, they most generally inspected that bridge. There was an old fellow named Woodruff. He nearly always went down and looked about that. Then the train men should have went and inspected that, but nobody did. Nobody reported, so the train went into it there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the main work that you had done on the section? What was the work like when you were working on it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, they done it with picking shovels. They had a jack to raise a track with when it was down. But the work was done with picking shovels. It wasn't like it is now. They've got some kind of machinery to do pretty much everything. It sounds like the way it used to be done back when they first started laying the track. Yeah, back the same way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't see it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, I didn't. It's somewhere else among my papers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it real hard work? Was it the kind of work that would really tire a man out?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, it was tired of work most of the time. Yet they kind of made fun of railroad men. They took it easy because they nooned at eleven o 'clock.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They nooned at eleven o 'clock?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, we went to work at seven, worked four hours, laid off at eleven, and then went to work at twelve again. And there would be a laying around, you know, and fellas going along. And railroad men, they don't do nothing. Then at another time, you know, you'd be out there and there'd be a train a -coming. Well, you had to keep a section. Men had to keep out of the way of that. They couldn't get on and go somewhere when that train was coming. Well, they might have to wait an hour or two someplace. People see them there, little men do nothing. So a lot of some young fellas turned and went to work on the railroad, thought it was awful easy. And our old boss that we had there, he said when they got new men, he'd say, Now, men, break them in right. He says, Just go right to work and just keep a -going. Don't stop. Stop for a tall, just break them in right, he says. There was one young fella, two young fellas went to work there. One worked quite a while the other and worked three or four days. And he'd throw it down and he'd shovel nobody but a dirty stone. So it worked on the railroad.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: The boss would tickle him big as he'd have had away an own. He told his young fella, Dad, about it. Well, the old man had made him mad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He says, Why didn't you beat hell out of him?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He says, Here, you worked on the railroad all your life. And then some little warden like that would come along and make such a crack as that. He said, I'd beat hell out of him.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Now, sometimes the work didn't seem to be hard. They had to clean the track of weeds. A lot of times, you know, folks would slide along the rail there and tick all the weeds. And then the bridges, they had to be weeded. Well, now, that run into work when you sat there four hours humped up on the railroad track and kept pulling weeds along. And then they had to cut fire guard along the hills to keep the fire from getting a train going up the hill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's a fire guard?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They cut a place about that wide to cut all the grass off. So the fire would burn in and you'd catch some railroad, it'd burn up to that, and most likely it'd stop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About how many feet would you have to have away from the rail? How far would you have to have to have?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, we'd be out there 50 feet lots of times from the track. That's pretty far. Right out there. Clear out the edge of the right of way. But one day, I was cutting guard down in a rocky place. They had to pull it. You couldn't cut the thing. I pulled up something and threw dirt in my eyes. Boy, my eyes watered there for a little while until I couldn't see. And I got so I could see again, I went and pulled at the upper side of my place I was pulling. I started down the lower side to pull again and looked over in the grass, about that far from the edge of the grass. There was a rattlesnake just like that big handful in there. I looked over there and seen him. My brother -in -law seen me. He was working here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, you seen him?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: You sure hit him quick when you seen him. I rattlesnake the snakes out of the way. And if he comes along, if I've got anything and see it, I hit it before I think. I don't stop and look at it and think, well, I'd better hit it. I hit it. I hit it. I hit it. I hit it. But that don't run near a bunch of men like pulling weeds and finding one of these little fellas about like your finger, about that long in the grass. Now that takes all the nerve out of a crew. I believe me. Run on one of them little ones. Generally, the boss picks the men up and takes them somewhere else for a jiggle. They're a nerve saddle again. Them little things, you know, you could pick right, put almost, well, they could be in a little bunch of grass, you know, and you just take them right in your fingers. They're just a little.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you really shook a crew up? Would they be afraid that the big ones were around too? Well, just them nerves and thinking about them little, they might be, you know, another little one there, isn't he?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: The big ones, they didn't bother a fella much. He killed one of them. The little ones were hard to see. They were hard to see and they're so likely to be right where you, you know, in a little bunch of grass, right where you'd practically pick them up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was the, was this, what was the railroad? Was it NP? Was that the?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: It was CP. That was a part of the Northern Pacific and the UP. The UP, they had a track partnership and they took that and organized, called it Camas Prairie. That two pieces of road made a different, a different company out of it. They had the headquarters near Lewiston. Camas Prairie went up the headquarters there. Camas Prairie. That one line went up the headquarters and then it went down, down the river around Rapier somewhere that way. And then another line went up to Grangeville. That was the Camas Prairie road there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the railroads, the management of the railroads like? I mean, were those, were they, did they care very much about the men? I mean, were they, did they do much for the men or was it mostly all the union that?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, yeah, we were treated, treated pretty good there.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: The worst we ever treated was a time they wouldn't give us any raise, any hourly raise when other wages did. Give us 11 hours time for 10 hours work. Afterwards though they got, Afterwards they got, afterwards that day, oh well, when I went to work I only got three and a half, three and a half a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I was just coming out of the Depression, right?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I mean, that was still in the 30s. Well, listen, I want to ask you a little bit more about when you're growing up. All of a sudden we went up to the 30s, which is pretty recent.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were, when you were growing up, you grew up up on the, on Fix Ridge? Is that where you grew up?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, no. When we come here there was no schools down the canyon here. And my brother was a teacher in school in Lapway. I think he had, my brother Albert, he had 80 or 90 pupils over there. He was one teacher, eighth grade. And he got $65 a month.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: So, he rented a place over there. My sister and I went from down the Catholic Gulf to Spalding a little while to school. And then my brother rented a place up there at Lapway and took my sister and myself and two brothers and two cousins up there. And we stayed there and went to school to him at Lapway. For one winter. That was in, that was in two, fall of two and the spring of three.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that, what was that like? Were they mostly Indian kids at school?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, there was, they had an Indian school there. They had an Indian school there. Only three Indians went to the white school there. But, oh, they had the Indian school around the other side. And they didn't have much, well, we didn't really have much to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Only the funny thing was, having the separate schools right away, any time that two or three whites or just a few whites was out and a bunch of Indians come along, hello, you want to fight?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, of course there's more of them. They wouldn't say nothing if there's less. But they would want to fight. There's more of them than there was the whites. But they was putting anybody that way. There was a fella,

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: McIntyre, his folks run a mill over there at that time. And he was out working on a mill race and it was dark. He come by and a whole bunch of them, there must have been about 20, very big Indians come out of there. He's come along alone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They said, hello, you want to fight?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, he said, I don't think so. Well, they said, come over here. He went over there and they formed a ring around him and jerked him up, jerked out the knife. He said, you've got to fight. Well, he said, if you're going to do that, I guess I can too. And he pulled a gun. He said, you ought to see him scatter when he pulled back.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: Then he went to school, what, Arrow, down here at Pilot Rock and some on fixed ridge?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Then I went to school the next year. They got this school built down there where this concrete, it has a wooden house there then. But I went to school there that year and then we bought the place on the fixed ridge. Then I went up there then, onto the fixed ridge school. Oh, listen, I just want to ask you, you went to school that way now that you, there was 80 kids or so in the... One teacher, eight grades and one teacher.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it in one room there?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: One room school, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you learn anything, 80 kids in the class?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, yes. It sounds like a rough way. It sounds pretty rough on your brother too, I imagine. Well, it was. And there was some big boys there that took a notion they'd whip a teacher time or two. But they never made it all work out. But he went and grew up, you know, just from his nine years of flat -blading and spalling and schooling. We lived on the fixed ridge all one year and my mother got awful sick up there. So after that we'd go up there and pick the stock up there and stay till about Christmas. After Christmas we'd come back down to my sister's place. So I most always went to school about half a year up there and then come back down here in the canyon. And then arrow school down there was closest and I went down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was she sick with?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, I don't know. Just the weather didn't too cold for her up there. It was just kind of too rough to live up there for her. Oh, it was quite a little colder and damper up there than it was down here. She lived to be 84 and then dad 86. Yeah, they both died here in this house. This was their house, place. This used to be their place here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it kind of rough to get up to the top, to get up the down fixed ridge?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, it used to be. In the good old muddy days, just mud roads. They'd take four horses on a hat or a two wheeled cart or anything like that. Lots of times them ranchers would stay here in town. It would be a struggle whether they'd stay in a hotel here in town rather than try to go home in the evening, late evening.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah. And they'd follow the travelled horse back. They'd bring their eggs to town in a big basket on the arm. Yeah. I used to carry eggs. I used to carry eggs from up there to town. I had a big horse with my brothers one time and a basket of eggs on my arm. Riding along slow. Come by a bunch of buck brush and just christened that there and there's a calf stuck its head out. Scared that horse and boy, she started right now. Well, I had that of eggs against the saddle there in the horse. I had that this way, I throwed my arm out this way. I only broke 18 out of the basketful until I got up and got her stopped. Then I used to have a spotted pony on my own. I used to carry eggs from up there to town on her. And she never would stand still to get on. She'd always get on. I'd always get on her on the run and I'd take a bucket of eggs and set them on a fence post. And slit the bale and make a bucket of bales stand up. Go back out the road a rod or two and grab the saddle on one of them and cut the strip and slice her head. Swing in the saddle with her on the run and go by and pick up my eggs and go on to town. They had the ferry boat in down below the Spalding Bridge. And most people traveled across on the railroad bridge there. You know, they get across the river, the railroad bridge that's still there. They had a cross band in that old bridge at that time there. They had it fixed so they couldn't come up there with boats. But there never was a boat come up after the railroad was put in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, so what about the ferry? Did that have much traffic going across the river on it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, there was quite a bit there. We never went over very many times. They would go. Well, it was a doctor and laugh way. And there was quite a bit of business, you know. You could get grain and all. And there was quite a few that would take their tins and rigs, you know, and go across on the ferry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it rough for the farmers to get their harvest crops down here from the ridge? Was that pretty steep with the wagons and all?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, yes, it was pretty steep. And then the wagon and horses, they... It made them get up and go if they ever made it. Three miles away, our place was three miles away, and they used to make two loads one day and three the next from there. And Clark and Hall, they lived just two miles up. They'd make three loads a day to town. The others only made two with this back in their father.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Juliet has changed so much since I came out here in 1907. And gee, you know, there was stores, three or four stores, and Witcher Shops, and Jewelry Shops, and Doctor's Office, and Dennis. You just can't imagine the change that's been in Juliet since 1977. Yeah, Jewelry Store, Barber Shop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a lot more people living in the town and around the town back then, or was it about the same population?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't know of much of a population. And then they had a farm mill here, too. But the farmers all came in with their teams, you know, from on the ridge, and they hauled their grain and shipped out from down here at the railroad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was one person owned the warehouse at the time who shipped the grain?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, there was a kind of company, I think, that had the warehouse.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: There was one farmer come down one time off of that fixed ridge up there with a load of grain, and they had a warehouse right down here at the, like, down next to the railroad down there. And he stopped down there and unloaded his load of grain, started up his team to go out, and the tongue fell out of his wagon.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: The queen pin had broken, too, somewhere along, but it held in there until he stopped and slacked off of it, and then, a few days later, he started to move again.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: Have you ever talked to Otto Schufer?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I'm planning to. We did talk to Herman Schufer while he was still living. Otto can tell a whole lot about, you know, still on. Juliaetta was... Yeah, he knows more about Juliaetta than I do. It's just a town in here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you folks come to town, this town, much, when you were growing up or when you were young, you know, just young adults? Did you use the town much?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, quite often, they'd be trading both. Well, at one time, they had a store down there there that I thought could get flour and sugar and things like that. They were a long time, and that kept them from coming to Juliaetta a lot of times. I used to... Well, we had to come to Juliaetta or Spalding. Yeah, we went down there from down that arrow way, from down that way, while they went to Spalding, but from up above Arrow, while they come to Juliaetta here, or else traded there with Kress at Arrow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One time, I took my cream down that arrow, and Judge Kress come out and told me, he says, what do you got to do after you put your cream on?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I told him, nothing. Well, he says, I've got a phone message here for Walter McCanter. I asked how far he lived, and I done about a quarter of a mile. Well, he says, I'll put your cream on the train if you'll take this phone message up to Walter. I said, all right. So I set my can of cream in the shade of a little building over there, and when the train come in, my Kress run a lunch counter there, but he hikes out there to put my cream on the train. He looked around. He didn't see it. I said, well, the train man had set it on, and he went out there for three days again. He come back, and he looked right behind that little building, and there set my can of cream. I went down that day with another can, and he told me, he said, I bet you're getting mad at me because I left that set there. He says, you send it on in, and I'll pay you whatever the difference is. Well, I put the two cans on and sent them in. They both went in at the same price. There wasn't any difference. That one had set there in the hot sun for three days.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: It's a wonder, too, wasn't it, hadn't it got hot enough to blow the lid off of it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't know. Maybe them three days wasn't specially hot. I never paid any attention, but anyway. We had good water. It wasn't cold water, but we had a spring house there in a place we could set a ten -gallon can clear down to the handles in the water. That's where we kept our cream as a place there.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That used to be quite a business, that dairy. Everybody had some cows, and that's the biggest laugh.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They had their cows, and they put them in the most of them. Some of them had water in the corral, but a lot of them had dry corrals. Milked them out, and no barns to milk in. Most of them didn't have. Some few did. Well, up on the ridges, they had places they could milk them in the barn when it was nasty in the winter, but they milked the corral in the summertime. Walked out there and milked the cows anywhere.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Then they didn't feed the cows. They went out in the dry pasture and picked their grass, picked their feed, and then stood in the dry corral all night, and then it turned out the next morning and had to go pick in the hot part of the day, they had to get their... I don't know how they ever give any milk at all. That's rougher than I kind of thought it used to be for that. Yeah, I think about that now. The dairy cow, she's fed while she's being milked, and she's fed all the time after that, and they're just fed up, fed good all the time. But them dairy cows, they milked them days. It was kind of rough to raise the hay even. Just along the little clear places along the hills and so on, and drag them hay down on a homemade sled. Yeah, maybe you couldn't drag it down at all. You had to take cows up to it.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, the idea seemed... I get the idea that they wanted to have as many different things as they could. I mean, they had orchards and cows as well as crops, and a lot of different diverse... Oh, yeah, they all kept a few chickens and hogs. They kind of traded work back and forth, you know, and there wasn't much money.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And traded your eggs at the store for what you got out of the store?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Eggs used to be about ten cents a dozen. Not often eggs. You only stayed about two bits in this country here. Well, we sold them down at Christmas for fifteen. Well, sometimes they got down to fifteen. They'd buy a loaf of bread for five cents. Yes, and a great big sack of tobacco for five cents.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that most folks were just barely getting by, just trying to barely get a living? Or were many people doing very well in those first years of the century?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I mean, nowadays a lot of people seem to be doing quite well on farms. No, well, yes. No, I guess the dining room and the kitchen was all together. But here's a room where she cut the mica, and then the dining room and the kitchen, and then her and my bedroom was all... just three of those three rooms there. And she was... The mica that was just mined out wasn't solid like this big bunch that I just told you about. It came out just in pieces. And it was ragged edges, and she just had a knife and she just cut off those ragged edges and laid out the good mica, you see. That was just mica dust that had to be cleaned up every day. But she worked in the dining room there and did that. And they just... The mica was ragged, and she just had a knife and she just... Supposedly this was just all sticking out here and some loose. She'd just do like, kind of like this. She'd kind of trim it. Trim it to make it more even, so that all this dust and stuff wouldn't go. And then put that out. This is the better mica, you see. And this mica dust, of course, had to be cleaned up and just taken off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she the only cutter that they had working in the mine?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, yeah. And we were the only two women up there. All the rest of the men were... All the rest were men that worked in the mine. So they brought mica into her and she just... They had a cart and a track laid back in the mica mine. And they just filled out of that and brought it in and brought in the mica. And brought in an amount for her to work on each day. And so she just kind of shaved off the edges so it was better. There might be several pieces of thicknesses here now. She just kind of shaved off those rust edges and put it into a better... For it was better mica.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was her name? Do you remember?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, I know. Because she was Monica Peterson.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I went with her brother for nine years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she from Avon too?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, she was in Avon. Avon's two girls. And that was work that women could do.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what about the cooking that you did? How many were you cooking for?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Was it a big... I think we had about six men maybe. I think maybe that many. Seems to me there were about that many.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Three meals a day?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh yes, sure. Oh, I've done lots of cooking like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I'm just curious how long at a time did you stay up there?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, they just mined enough to work... Now I can't tell them... To work out the assessment on them. That's all the time they worked. And those mines didn't operate. They would have been paid off if they would have operated off longer I guess. But of course they got to using something else and sit them like this. It wasn't needed so much. And I don't know the nature of that now. Some of the lunchrooms could tell you more about that than I know. But I just worked up there when they were working. I was at the Luella mine and then the other one above it several miles... Or several up the hill where there was the mosque. I remember one time... One evening... We, from where I was working, from the mine where we were working... All walked up the hill and had a dance in their dining room... Well, the dining room and kitchen all together, you know. Had a dance up there. Our music was mouth organ. We walked up that hill, climbed up that hill and kind of slid back down. Of course you couldn't hardly keep your footing. And you asked about getting groceries up there. When we wanted groceries there was always a team of horses that would go... Now I don't know. This is kind of blurry of me about the team of horses. But how we got them. But once we... When this girl and I were working there and we had just gone up there... We didn't have mirror or anything. So we wanted a mirror. And so the boss, Charlie Lacy, said, well you take the team and go down to Troy... And get the groceries. And you do what shopping you want to do. So I've got the mirror in there now that I bought it. And I paid a dollar and a half for it. It's lovely, great being there about so long. And it's a nice one. And so we got the groceries and came home. And oh the awfulest rainstorm and the horses weren't shod. And to come up and climb that hill with that wagon of horses... And we had groceries. We had flour and everything. And we were wet. Oh we were wet and cold. And we drove that team up there and sometimes they'd slip... Slide around in that mud going in the hill. I thought we'd never get up. And when they'd get tired pulling we'd have to slam on the brake and block the wheels. Oh that was an awful trip. But she and I made that trip to Troy to do some shopping that we needed to do... And to bring back the load of groceries. Other than that some of the men went. And I don't remember about where the horses came from or whether they got a... One of the men that worked up there lived at what we call the foot of the hill... Down in the Avon country. And they might have got his team to drive out. I don't know. I don't remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it two horses and a wagon?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, two horses and a wagon. Uh huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of pay did you get for doing that?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I can't tell you. I can't remember what I got. But... Oh I don't know. It might have been a dollar a day. Something like that. It wasn't much. Or it might have... I don't... It might have been fifty cents a day. I don't know. It wasn't much but it was the wages they were paying in those days. But I don't remember really what wages I got. Sounds like when you had to cook out there before you had a cook house. That must have been... Well I think it wasn't... I don't think I had to cook out there. It was just a little shack of a stove. Two sheet iron stoves. But so happened I didn't have to... I guess we must have bought bread or something. I don't know. I don't remember those details. But anyway I cooked out over the shed until they got the cook house and room built.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did you think you moved into Deery?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: When Earl and I were married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you and you meet by the way?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: At a dance. And a funny thing. I worked one time for the axel. And on Halloween... The young girl there, Alice... Hall. She was the granddaughter of the axel. But on Halloween we were peeling apples and we were saying, look at the moon over your right shoulder and your wish will come true and things like that. And we peeled apples and they were supposed to put them around your head like this. And then wherever they fall, well that will be the initial...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The initial of the person you marry?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: The initial of the last name of the person you're going to marry. And on that Halloween night I had the... Oh I had a dream that I saw the most handsome young fellow I saw. Oh he was handsome. And when I met Earl... That was the fellow I saw in my dream. That's a fact. Now that's a fact. I hadn't met him when you had the dream. I hadn't met him when I twirled this apple around here and all and made my wishes and things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get an initial G when you twirled the apple around?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't remember about that but I... The dream that I had, I saw this young fellow at a dance. And the minute I saw Earl I knew that was the fellow I think. Now I know that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you still going with young Peterson at that time?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes at that time.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well nine years is a pretty long courtship. I imagine even in those days it is. Yes it was. But we just never got around to getting married. He finally said to me, well 19 -8 or never Kate, but we never got married. I went with him a little. He was a fine fellow. Schoolmate. Knew him for years and years and years. Did you and Mr. Brannis, you met at the dance and it sounds like you might have fell for each other right away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did Earl say about that one time?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, he told me afterwards. Now we've talked about this when he first went up to the home. We kind of went over things like this. And he told me once that no, when he first met me he never thought of marrying me. And then when he wanted to kid me later, after we'd gone together a number of years, several years. He tells everybody else I had to marry her to get rid of her. He's quite a joker. He's full of fun. But when I met him, I knew that that was a fellow that I had seen in my dreams. I just knew that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go together for very long before you were married?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh I don't remember. A couple of years I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get shivareed?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes. No, no. Instead of having a shivaree they gave us a shower. When we came back from our wedding trip, they gave us a shower at Derry and took up a collection and raised fifty dollars and we bought a stove with them. And Chuck Wells, you know who Chuck Wells is, wasn't at the dance. But the next time he saw Earl, he took a dollar out of his pocket and he said, I didn't get to put my contribution in so you take this he said. So he gave Earl a dollar. All the rest of the fellows I guess were giving it out that we bought our stove with.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you moved right into Derry then?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, we moved right into Derry when we were married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And was he working for Potlatch at the time?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, he wasn't working for the Potlatch. He was running the livery barn and a pool hall. He and another fellow, he and Frank Holbrook. Frank wasn't in on the livery barn but Frank was in with him on the pool hall. They had a pool hall there and Earl run the livery barn and he's a veterinarian and he practiced.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was a vet then? Where he had him?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, he has taken care of lots of the farmers, hogs and horses and cows. So they always call him Doc Ranice. That's where he got his name, Doc Ranice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had he just come into the area at that time when Derry started up or had he been there before?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He came to Derry from Helmer. His people were located up at Helmer and there are some of those in the picture there. And he was with them until they moved down to Derry. He came out when they were still at Helmer. Then they all moved down to Derry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He came out from?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: From the east. The east. I've forgotten what state now. Iowa I guess. Well, you mentioned Chuck Wells and I just got to ask you some of those recollections of Wells' family. I've danced with Chuck and Roy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Danced with Chuck and Roy. Now I was going to... In their home I guess or either in their home or...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would it be?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I've danced with both of them.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: My father and mother had the whole Wells family at our house for dinner more than one Sunday and Mary carried me in her arms. I was small enough that she could pick me up and carry me. And they've been to dinner in our folks' home. So they were pretty good friends with your parents. Those people were treated just as any white person when they came. In the early days nobody shunned those darkies at all. Not at all. They were in different families' homes. I've been in their homes... Well, when we lived at Derry we went up there one night and had a dance. Joe was drinking and enjoying himself but we danced at their house. But now that was... I think that was after Mary was gone from there. I think the Derry bunch went up there and I think just Joe and his wife, Lou, were there. Because I don't remember ever going after when Mary was up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You say in the early days, do you think it changed then as years went on?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh yes. Oh yes.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Then when Mary's child was born, I don't know how many people of Derry went up to see her right away. Because they were all curious. Because they said they tell me that a little darkie child is white when it's born. I didn't go up and I don't know the result or anything about it. But I know people were curious. It's white and then it turns dark. Well, that's what some of them said but now I don't know about that at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you think that the attitude changed and they really weren't as well accepted as time were they?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, different people come in and then they never thought of the Wells. Like they didn't take them in like the early day people did. The early day people, that is, our family and several that I know of, just visited with them the same as we visited with white folks. Well, they came in with the Wells boys, you know, who were carpenters there and took the name of Wells because they had been, I think they were the Wells' father's slaves. I think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't I tell you that before?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, and I've heard that elsewhere.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Have you?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, yes. They came there and my father thought a great deal of the Wells boys, they were blacksmiths and Joe and Mary came, the family came there with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what do you remember Lou as being like? Joe's wife?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't know. She was well thought of as far as I know and I don't remember anything about her housekeeping or anything but it seems to me that I remember her once kind of dancing a little dance step in our dance hall up there or something. Now it seems to me I remember that but I won't say sure. Well, there was a bachelor that lived out there in, you know, it's between Derry and Avon. It's nearer Derry than Avon. And Phil Clements was a, I don't know whether I should tell you this or not. Well. Well, anyway.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't know if I should.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why not?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: You don't have to use a name if you don't want to. Well, let's say there was a fellow that had married through a matronial paper and he and his wife didn't get along and he threatened her some way and she, I was away from home when this happened but the girl said, my tour about it and his wife came to our house to stay a while and then she went, she went back and I don't know how he threatened again in some way and another neighbor had come to get her to take her away or take her back home now, which it was, I don't know. And he was there and he shot her and then she,

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: no. I better not tell. Anyway, he shot himself in the home then. He knew he'd done what he'd done and he shot himself in the home. And they didn't know what all had happened there and a bunch of fellows from Derry went out there armed to take him into custody or something and he had farmed, he was farming and he had planted grain right up close around the house. And they were creeping up to the house and Chuck Welch was one of the fellows that was looking, spying to this place and of course this is a joke about Chuck. And they said that the door was open and he creeps up kind of close and the door had been open quite a while and evidently the chickens went in and the chicken jumped down off the table and then when they said Chuck's pregnant he turned white. He was so frightened. The joke is about niggers, you know, that if they get frightened they'll turn white. So that was the joke about that. I was going to ask you about that killing because Kent Wilkins told me that you would be the person who would most likely know the truest account of what had happened because I've heard two different accounts of that killing. That this man, what I heard was that the woman was kind of, she was being threatened by him quite a bit after they were married. She had been threatened about him. He married her through a matrimonial paper and she had been threatened until she was afraid to stay there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well didn't she come down to stay at your place?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, she was at our house for a while. We were young, I wasn't at home, I was away from home when this happened, but she came down and stayed at our house for a while. I don't know how long. And then Billy Bower I think took her home finally and that's when he shot her, you know. He shot her when she was getting out of the wagon. The buggy that he had taken her home in. And then of course shot himself. And Earl was one of the groups that went down with guns and crap around there to find her. And of course they didn't know that he might shoot them, they didn't know he was dead, you see. But that was the joke, the door was open and when a chicken jumped down off the table, Chuck was near the door and they said he could near turn white. So it was frightenful. There, Pupi, Mox, Mox and Pete. Pupi had a bunch of cattle and Pete went away somewhere and Pete made a big eat while he was gone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A big what?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well they called it a big eat, they had a powwow, a big eat. And he butchered some of Pupi's cattle and had this big eat. Pupi come back, he's pretty mad. He says, Jesus up there, maybe no Jesus up there now, maybe me kill Pete.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Chuck 7th, he was a good and jolly Indian and he was honest. But if you made a mistake in dealing with him, he wouldn't come and make a right to your mistake. So, there was her stepdad had never seen a lynx cat near a, under a lynx. So he was a buying fur and these lynx cats just had a few long hairs go straight up on top of their ears. I never seen a lynx but they were supposed to have kind of a tousle up there some way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So Jack he brought out this fur out there and her stepdad said, oh Jack you got a lynx?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, he said, he's got a lynx. A lynx was five dollars and a bobcat was three and a half. So Wilson he paid him five dollars for it and when returns come, they paid him for a bobcat, three and a half. He lost a dollar and a quarter on the deal. My dad, he seen Jack a while after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, Jack, you sold Wilson a bobcat for a lynx?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, Jack said, a little one too. He said, Wilson ought to know better. Well there was a fur buyer and bought a bobcat a lynx cat hide fur lynx. So the Indian, he got a lot of fun out of getting and beating him out a dollar and a quarter.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well P .O. Vitalik, we liked him pretty well. We had some cattle up in the timber one year, took in cattle and pasture. Yeah, we took in pasture and he had some cattle up in there. So he come up to help ride in the fall and he stayed at our cabin and we fed his horse, you know, fed him. Also we fed the other riders too, but they didn't consider it anything but what we should do again. And when P .O .P. left, why, he come to me and he handed me some money. And of course they'd be selling it in for the pasture.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, why, what's that for?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And he always said, good eat, good sleep. I go to town today. He said, I give more, but I get to town and have, anyway, didn't have too much money. But anyway, he thanked us. I seen P .O .P. I've seen pretty much of him and the rest of them never even said anything. But P .O .P., I seen him several times and he always had a lot of tear things on his coats, you know, and a bottle of beads around his neck and the way he dressed. I thought he was a kind of a crazy old boy. But when he come up there and stayed at our place, I found out that he was a mighty smart fellow. He wouldn't use no tobacco, no booze, no coffee. He said, maybe old man, lots of years. He lived to be old too. He was, oh boy, he was a nice old fellow when you got acquainted with him. Them younger mox -moxes, the ones that had their mother was, well they wasn't really, but they was George and Tom.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: George, he was educated, highly educated. Carlisle graduate.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What graduate?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, that's it. Carlisle College. He was well educated, but he got to be an alcoholic. And when he got drunk, oh, he was mean. He beat up on his wife and threw her in the river and he chased his mother off the place. Then he was in the pen when we come to the country, but he come out and George, George tie up with his wife while he was gone. He had two children and George and her had two when he got back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he asked Lee Hall, he says, what relation? He said, would my children be to George's from the same mother?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They had two brothers, you know, and the same woman, same mother. Lee said, I don't really know. Well, he said, I guess it's all right, but I don't like it very damn well. But he got to drinking again after he come back and he burned up one house, burned a dog up, built another one. He got drunk and got in that and he burned himself up in that house. But he, an Indian wouldn't tell who, where they got booze, they'd die before they'd tell a white man sold it to them. He laid up the draw there and he seen George coming one day and he knowed he was drunk, so he stopped to see him. George come up and he went to talking to him and when he drank with him, they didn't get him to drink and he was getting pretty drunk. And gentleman said he'd get him to talk in big and he'd think, well now's about time to ask him where he got that. Should have seemed like George could just read his mind. When he'd think, now I'm going to ask him where he got that, he'd laugh and say, you white man don't catch him. No, he never did, never did get to ask him where he got that booze. I wonder why they figured that way, because they didn't want to, they were afraid that the guy would get caught. Oh yeah, he'd get pinched if they squealed on him. He wouldn't get any more booze from him either. But he never would tell, he died, burned up from drinking booze. And he was a nice, well educated fellow and when he was drunk, why he was nice, nice man to talk to and everything, just well educated. You know, I've heard that the way you can handle booze or whether or not it would turn you into an alcoholic has something to do with how long the people have been exposed to it. And that a lot of the European people especially, they've had it in Europe for so long, wine and all that. But the Indian people, having never really had it before, didn't ever build up a tolerance to it. Yes, that was a whole lot of it, that they couldn't handle it as well as a white man. Well, a lot of white men went wild with it too, but then most generally two of them get too drunk and get in a fight, one of them would kill the other. And there was an awful lot of white men that got too much booze and got killed. I've heard that Indians, if they were your friend, it really meant something. Well, there was, they were fine people. They were honest.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: You know, the white men had pulled them down to his level and ruined them.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He's the only one left of his family. There was 11 in the family, three of them, two of them were just infants when they died. And there was one about 18 months old. Eight lived to be grown. And he was the baby of the family and he's the only one left now. Everyone of his folks are gone. So it seems kind of lonesome when you think about it, you know, in all these years. And when there was a big family of them, we all were around together. And now then, the last four years, I think, since Hugh's left, he's the only one left.
